answer:
**Charles Dunoyer's closing chapter (Chapter 19 of *Nouveau Traité d'Économie*, Vol. II) is more programmatic than Charles Comte's (Chapter 27 of *Traité de Législation*, Vol. II).**[^1][^2]

Comte's chapter concludes his volume with an ethnographic analysis of social organization, hierarchies, and morals among tropical West African ("Ethiopian species") peoples, drawing parallels to feudal Europe, Malay islanders, and other "primitive" societies observed earlier in the volume.[^3] It emphasizes empirical observation of inequalities, slavery, gender roles, justice systems (e.g., poison ordeals), and conquest-based hierarchies, ending on a comparative note about relative security and subsistence between these agricultural negro societies and more barbaric southern African hordes.[^4] While insightful on human "degradation" and social evolution—the volume's broader theme—this finale is descriptive and analytical, not prescriptive. It offers no explicit agenda for reform, policy, or future action; instead, it wraps up racial/moral typology as a natural endpoint to Vol. II's geographic survey.[^5]

Dunoyer's chapter, by contrast, is a sustained manifesto for liberating "industries" (arts) of intellectual education—teaching, writing, printing—from state monopoly, poor methods (e.g., classical languages over practical sciences), and moral/civil vices.[^6] He programmatically applies his industrialist framework: education as cerebral "gymnastics" needing free markets, division of labor (specialized schools/professors), machines (press/telegraph), and moral habits (sobriety, anti-luxury, courage against censorship).[^7] He critiques monopolies (e.g., France's University control), demands full liberty in schools/journals, and celebrates progress via accumulated "capital" (e.g., rising print runs, literacy).[^8] The ending extrapolates to societal transformation—universal instruction, equality, moral softening—urging free intellectual markets as society's "soul" for all progress.[^9] This is forward-looking advocacy, a blueprint for liberal reform.

**Side-by-side, Dunoyer's programmatic thrust—reforming education as free industry for civilizational advance—far outpaces Comte's observational closure on primitive morals.** Comte diagnoses societal ills empirically; Dunoyer prescribes industrial liberty as cure.[^10]

[^1]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation*, Vol. II, Ch. 27, [translation](https://falsafa.corpus/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-ii-20331d/27/translation/).

[^2]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau Traité d'Économie*, Vol. II, Ch. 19, [translation](https://falsafa.corpus/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-ii-d6ae03/19/translation/).

[^3]: Ibid., Comte, ¶ [p-5a71e4]–[p-f35d7e] (social constitution, conquerors/vanquished).

[^4]: Ibid., ¶ [p-0ea169]–[p-f35d7e].

[^5]: Ibid., full chapter (no calls to action; "END OF THE SECOND VOLUME").

[^6]: Dunoyer, ¶ [p-2ac7c5]–[p-f92747] (education industries need liberty like manufacturing).

[^7]: Ibid., ¶ [p-464ecb]–[p-960d63] (business talent, methods, machines, division).

[^8]: Ibid., ¶ [p-742914]–[p-0061ea] (anti-monopoly, University critique); ¶ [p-570611]–[p-a58997] (stats on progress).

[^9]: Ibid., ¶ [p-d97b93]–[p-f35d7e].

[^10]: For TF-IDF-related chapters contrasting their styles, see tool pointers on "education/liberty" (Dunoyer) vs. "social organization/races" (Comte); neither overlaps programmatically.
